original_hypothesis.title: FeeSplitter Insolvency via Transfer-Based Fee Double-Claiming
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The _transfer function does not notify FeeSplitter (no onBalanceChange call). When tokens are transferred to a fresh address, the recipient's userFeeOffset is 0, allowing them to claim all historical fees. If the sender claims first, then transfers to a fresh address which claims again, the FeeSplitter pays out more than it holds, becoming insolvent and leaving other holders unable to claim.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a fundamental accounting flaw: the fee distribution system assumes offsets are synchronized with balance changes, but transfers bypass the synchronization entirely.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _transfer updates balances without calling feeRedistributor.onBalanceChange for sender or receiver

preconditions: Attacker holds curves tokens for a subject with accumulated holdersFees

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Recipient offset = 0; owed = cumulativeFeePerToken * balance — claims ALL historical fees
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: 1) Claim fees from address A. 2) Transfer tokens to fresh address B. 3) Claim from B (offset=0 → full historical fees). 4) Repeat with address C, D...
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Each cycle extracts cumulativeFeePerToken * tokenBalance worth of ETH until FeeSplitter is empty

economic_questions: How large is cumulativeFeePerToken after sustained trading activity?
How many cycles before FeeSplitter is fully drained?
Does .transfer() 2300 gas limit prevent re-entrancy during the drain?
validation_requirements: Simulate: claim → transfer → claim cycle and verify double payment
Calculate total extractable vs FeeSplitter balance

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost to buy initial tokens on bonding curve + gas
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for claim + transfer per cycle (~0.01 ETH per cycle)
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Multiple transactions, each cycle drains more

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After significant fee accumulation in FeeSplitter

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All token holders with unclaimed fees
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% loss of claimable fees once FeeSplitter is insolvent

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Typical cumulativeFeePerToken values and FeeSplitter balance
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace getClaimableFees for fresh address with offset=0

validation_result.mechanism: missing fee offset sync on transfer
validation_result.impact_summary: Token transfers bypass FeeSplitter notification, allowing recipients with offset=0 to claim all historical fees. Repeated claim→transfer→claim cycles can fully drain the FeeSplitter, leaving legitimate holders unable to claim.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The _transfer function in Curves.sol (lines 225-236) updates curvesTokenBalance for both sender and receiver but never calls feeRedistributor.onBalanceChange() for either party. In FeeSplitter.sol, a fresh address has userFeeOffset = 0 (Solidity mapping default). When the recipient calls claimFees, getClaimableFees computes owed = (cumulativeFeePerToken - 0) * balance, which equals ALL historical fees for that token amount since inception. The sender can claim their fees first (since their offset was properly set during their original buy), then transfer to a fresh address that claims again for the same historical period. This results in double-claiming that exceeds the actual ETH held by FeeSplitter.

Concrete example: Suppose 10 ETH in holder fees has been distributed for a token. User A holds all circulating tokens. A claims ~10 ETH (their full share). A transfers tokens to fresh address B. B claims ~10 ETH (offset=0 → full historical share). But only 10 ETH was ever deposited. After A's claim, only ~0 ETH remains, so B's claim (or a subsequent victim's claim) will fail due to insufficient balance in FeeSplitter.

The attack can be repeated: B claims, transfers to C, C claims, etc., each time attempting to extract the full historical fee amount.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Direct code inspection confirms the defect. The _transfer function at Curves.sol:225-236 contains no call to feeRedistributor.onBalanceChange(). The transferCurvesToken function at Curves.sol:218-220 calls _transfer without any additional FeeSplitter synchronization. In contrast, _transferFees (Curves.sol:186-214) DOES call feeRedistributor.onBalanceChange during buy/sell operations, proving the pattern is intended but was omitted from transfers. FeeSplitter.getClaimableFees (FeeSplitter.sol:73-78) uses userFeeOffset which defaults to 0 for addresses that have never interacted with the FeeSplitter, enabling the full historical fee claim.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: This function modifies token balances that FeeSplitter reads via curves.curvesTokenBalance(), but never calls feeRedistributor.onBalanceChange() for either the sender or receiver. The receiver ends up with tokens but userFeeOffset[receiver] remains 0, enabling them to claim all historical fees.

When userFeeOffset[account] is 0 (default for fresh addresses), owed equals cumulativeFeePerToken * balance, which represents ALL historical fees proportional to the balance. This enables the transferred-to address to claim fees from before they held any tokens.
Public entry point for token transfers. Only checks that the recipient is not the contract itself, then delegates to _transfer which lacks FeeSplitter notification.

This function sets the userFeeOffset to the current cumulativeFeePerToken, which would prevent historical fee claims if called during transfers. Its absence from the _transfer code path is the root cause of the vulnerability.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker (address A) calls claimFees(token) on FeeSplitter → updateFeeCredit sets A's offset to cumulativeFeePerToken, pays out accumulated fees, zeros unclaimedFees[A]
2. Attacker calls transferCurvesToken(subject, addressB, amount) on Curves.sol:218
3. _transfer (Curves.sol:225) executes: decrements A's balance, increments B's balance. NO call to feeRedistributor.onBalanceChange() for either A or B
4. Address B calls claimFees(token) on FeeSplitter.sol:80
5. updateFeeCredit (FeeSplitter.sol:65) computes: balance = B's new balance * PRECISION; owed = (cumulativeFeePerToken - 0) * balance (offset is 0!)
6. owed / PRECISION = full historical fees for B's token amount, added to unclaimedFees[B]
7. getClaimableFees returns unclaimedFees[B] which equals full historical fees
8. FeeSplitter sends claimable ETH to B via .transfer()
9. If FeeSplitter balance < claimable, the transfer reverts and other holders also cannot claim
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerability exists because Curves.sol:_transfer (lines 225-236) modifies curvesTokenBalance without synchronizing FeeSplitter offsets. Contrast with _transferFees (Curves.sol:210-211) which calls feeRedistributor.onBalanceChange(curvesTokenSubject, msg.sender) during buy/sell. The omission in _transfer means the FeeSplitter's accounting invariant (that offset tracks when a user started holding tokens) is violated. The fresh recipient's offset defaults to 0 in the Solidity mapping (tokensData[token].userFeeOffset[account]), and getClaimableFees (FeeSplitter.sol:73-78) computes owed from this 0 offset, yielding the full cumulativeFeePerToken * balance amount.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment configuration affects this vulnerability. The defect is in the core transfer logic.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation:
Attack is straightforward and requires no special configuration:
1. Attacker buys N tokens of a subject that has accumulated holder fees (cumulativeFeePerToken > 0)
2. Attacker calls claimFees() to collect their share — offset set to current cumulative
3. Attacker calls transferCurvesToken(subject, freshAddress, N) — _transfer updates balances without FeeSplitter notification
4. From freshAddress, attacker calls claimFees() — offset is 0, so full historical fees are computed
5. FeeSplitter pays out, potentially exceeding its balance
6. Repeat with more fresh addresses to drain completely

The attack cost is just gas + the initial token purchase cost (which can be recovered by selling tokens later). The profit is the entire FeeSplitter balance for that token.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL. The FeeSplitter can be fully drained by any token holder who performs a claim→transfer→claim cycle. All other token holders lose their unclaimed fees permanently. The attack is repeatable until the FeeSplitter is empty. With multiple subjects, the attacker can drain fees across all tokens. The attack requires only gas costs and temporary token holding.
recommended_mitigations: Add feeRedistributor.onBalanceChange() calls in _transfer for BOTH sender and receiver before balance modifications: feeRedistributor.onBalanceChange(curvesTokenSubject, from); feeRedistributor.onBalanceChange(curvesTokenSubject, to);
Fix onBalanceChange to call updateFeeCredit before resetting the offset (separate bug but compounds this issue)
Add the same FeeSplitter notifications to withdraw() and deposit() which also use _transfer without FeeSplitter sync
Consider adding a reentrancy guard to claimFees and transfer functions as defense-in-depth
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy Curves + FeeSplitter + Factory with fee percentages including holdersFeePercent > 0. 2. Subject S creates curve (buys token #1). 3. User A buys 5 tokens, generating holder fees via addFees. 4. User B buys 5 tokens, generating more holder fees. 5. Record FeeSplitter balance and cumulativeFeePerToken. 6. User A calls claimFees — records payout amount. 7. User A calls transferCurvesToken to fresh address C. 8. Address C calls claimFees — records payout amount. 9. Verify C's payout equals full historical fees (not just fees since transfer). 10. Verify total payouts to A + C exceed what A alone should have received. 11. Attempt another claim from User B — may fail if FeeSplitter is now insolvent.
uncertainties: The exact FeeSplitter balance depends on how many fee-generating trades occurred, but the vulnerability mechanism is certain
The .transfer() 2300 gas limit in claimFees prevents reentrancy during the attack but does not prevent the multi-transaction attack pattern
additional_testing_needed: Test with multiple subjects to verify cross-token drainage
Test withdraw/deposit paths which also use _transfer without FeeSplitter notification
Quantify the maximum extractable value relative to FeeSplitter balance
Test edge case where FeeSplitter runs out of ETH mid-claim (reverts vs partial payment)
